narration: One-third of people earning $250,000 still run out of money before payday. Wild, right? There's a hidden reason why more income doesn't automatically mean more savings.
visual: Large '36%' stat in amber, person in suit with $250K salary badge, empty wallet below — dark background

narration: It's called Parkinson's Law of Money — your expenses always expand to match your income. Get a raise, and somehow your lifestyle costs exactly that much more.
visual: Two bar charts on light background growing in sync: green INCOME bar and red EXPENSES bar climbing together, dashed amber line showing they always track each other

narration: Studies show Americans save roughly five to seven percent of income — no matter if they earn forty thousand or one-sixty thousand. The savings rate barely moves.
visual: Three income bars ($40K, $80K, $160K) growing taller on dark background, each with a tiny amber savings sliver at the top staying proportionally the same

narration: The culprit is status spending — bigger rent, nicer car, pricier restaurants. Each feels small, but together they swallow every extra dollar before you can save it.
visual: House, car, and fork/plate icons appearing one by one with price tags (+$900/mo, +$450/mo, +$350/mo), then '$1,700/MO CONSUMED' total — light background

narration: Wealthy people break this cycle by automating savings before lifestyle sees the money. They treat savings like a fixed bill — number one priority, always paid first.
visual: Flow diagram: PAYCHECK ARRIVES arrow pointing to SAVINGS FIRST badge then piggy bank — dark background with amber accents

narration: Here's the fix: every raise you get, automatically lock away half. Don't let lifestyle see it. That one habit compounded over time is worth seven figures.
visual: Raise arrow splitting 50/50 — coin stack labeled SAVED and house labeled SPENT — then '7 FIGURES' CTA badge springs in — light background
